questions.question: What is the baseline training time (in minutes) for GPT-2 124M on 8×H100 in Record 1 of the speedrun?
What is the exact training time (in minutes) achieved by Record 3, which introduced the Muon optimizer?
What are the three polynomial coefficients (a, b, c) used in the Newton-Schulz orthogonalization iterations within the Muon optimizer, and how many iterations are applied?
questions.gold_answer: a=3.4445, b=-4.7750, c=2.0315, applied for 5 iterations. The update rule is X_{k+1} = aX + bX^3 + cX^5.

questions.question: In the Muon optimizer, how are model parameters partitioned between the OrthogonalNesterov update and the AdamW update, and what specific learning rates are used for each group in the final configuration?
questions.gold_answer: Parameters with ndim >= 2 (weight matrices) receive OrthogonalNesterov updates with lr_muon=0.6; parameters with ndim < 2 (biases, norms, embeddings) receive AdamW updates with lr_adam_1d=0.04; the LM head has a separate AdamW group with lr_adam_head=0.008.

questions.question: What three conditions must all be satisfied for the FlexAttention document-aware mask to allow attention between a query token and a key-value token?
questions.gold_answer: Causal (query position >= kv position), same_doc (document IDs match), and in_window (query position minus kv position <= window size). The block mask is compiled with 128-token block granularity.

questions.question: Claim C03 states the Muon optimizer achieves the single largest record-to-record speedup. What is the percentage reduction in training time from Record 2 to Record 3, and how does this compare to the percentage reduction achieved during the entire Architecture phase (Records 5–8)?
questions.gold_answer: Record 2 to Record 3: from 36.83 min to 23.10 min, a reduction of approximately 37.3%. The Architecture phase (Records 5–8) reduced time from 21.70 min to 11.04 min, a reduction of 49.1%. So the Architecture phase achieved a larger cumulative percentage reduction, but Muon (Record 3 alone) is the single largest single-record reduction.

questions.question: FlexAttention is introduced in Record 11 with a sliding window of 1024 tokens. By which record is the sliding window reduced to 128 tokens, and what other architectural change is introduced in that same record?
questions.gold_answer: Record 15 reduces the sliding window to 128 tokens (block SW 128) and also introduces GQA (Grouped Query Attention) and gradient_as_bucket_view.

questions.question: Why does the Muon optimizer restrict Newton-Schulz orthogonalization to parameters with 2 or more dimensions, and what happens to 1D parameters instead?
questions.gold_answer: Orthogonal updates are only geometrically meaningful for matrix-valued parameters where Stiefel manifold structure exists. 1D parameters (biases, layer norms) lack this structure, so they are handled by a standard AdamW optimizer instead.

questions.question: What are the two dead ends documented in the exploration tree, what failure mode did each exhibit, and what lesson was recorded for each?
questions.gold_answer: Dead end N07a: cuDNN attention was slower than expected at short sequences (~4K tokens), only beneficial at 64K+ context; lesson: avoid premature optimization. Dead end N17a: removing 2 or more transformer layers caused validation loss regression despite skip connections; lesson: a minimum model depth exists that skip connections cannot compensate for.

questions.question: What is the exact wall-clock time overhead (in milliseconds or percentage) added by the Newton-Schulz orthogonalization step per optimizer iteration in the distributed 8×H100 setup, as measured in the paper's experiments?
questions.gold_answer: The paper states Newton-Schulz adds approximately 5% compute per optimizer step in the algorithm complexity section, but no exact measured wall-clock overhead in milliseconds for the distributed 8×H100 setup is reported in the provided material.
